1:
The Chronicler opens the second attempt to bring the ark home not with the procession but with a builder's quiet care, and the witnesses read David's preparations as the visible fruit of a hard lesson. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening clause "and he finished a palace for himself," the plural "houses" being an intensive for the one royal residence, and refers it back to the building of the house of cedar at 1 Chronicles 14:1; the "city of David" is the castle of Zion, and the place made ready for the ark lay probably within the palace precincts. The reformed John Gill agrees that the houses are the royal palace, with all its apartments and offices for so great a household, raised with the help of the masons and carpenters Hiram had sent. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds that the palace was a cluster of many houses for David's several wives and children, and draws a shrewd inference from the tent: David would not fetch the old tabernacle of Moses from Gibeon, because he already purposed to build the temple, and meant no permanent housing for the ark short of that.

The deeper design of the verse the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set out plainly. Where the parallel at 2 Samuel 6 narrates only the bare transfer of the ark and its setting up in a tent on Zion, the Chronicler elaborately portrays the religious side of the act — the preparations David made, and a full enumeration of the Levites who carried it out according to the precepts of the law; and this first verse records the erection of the tent in the city of David. Ellicott notes what the arrangement left standing: the old Mosaic tabernacle remained at Gibeon, where Zadok ministered, as 1 Chronicles 16:39 records, while Abiathar most likely ministered before the ark in the new tent David had pitched.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the preparation into devotion. David now prepared a place for the ark before he brought it up, and thus he sought God in the due order; having no time to build a house, he pitched a tent, as near the pattern shown to Moses as he could. And Henry fixes on the sequence: "when he made houses for himself in the city of David he prepared a place for the ark." Wherever we build for ourselves, he urges, we must be sure to make room for God's ark, for a church in the house. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sums the two buildings in a line — one house for himself and one for the ark, with a tent under which the ark was placed — so that the witnesses leave the reader with a king who housed his God before he settled at ease in his own palace.
2:
"None ought to carry the ark of God but the Levites" — David's ruling opens the reform, and the witnesses hear in it a public confession of the earlier fault. The reformed John Gill notes only that David said this, whether within himself or to the courtiers about him; but the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the resolution its full weight. David had determined that the Levites alone should bear the ark, because the LORD had chosen them for it, as the law had laid down in Numbers 4:15, Numbers 7:9, and Numbers 1:50. The little word "then," they add, marks the time — the end of the three months the ark had rested with Obededom — and by this arrangement it was expressly acknowledged that it had been contrary to the law to set the ark upon a cart at the first.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the same point of conscience. "Then" is a real note of time, closing the three months' interval; and the appeal to the law is drawn straight from Numbers 4:5 and the verses following, where the Kohathite Levites are appointed to carry the ark, and yet more definitely from Deuteronomy 10:8, where the LORD separated the tribe of Levi "to bear the ark of the covenant." David's careful enunciation of the rule, Ellicott observes, is a tacit acknowledgment that on the former occasion it had not been kept; and he notes that though the older account at 2 Samuel 6:13 states plainly that the ark was now carried by bearers, it does not trouble to say they were Levites — a detail the Chronicler is at pains to supply.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke drives the lesson home with a pastor's bluntness: the carrying of the ark was the Levites' proper business, and David should have thought of this sooner; had he done so, the unhappy breach upon Uzza would have been prevented, as the king himself will confess at v.13. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that this verse is the hinge of the whole chapter — the moment a chastened king returns to the written word he had once neglected, and orders the sacred things to be handled in God's appointed way rather than in the way that had seemed convenient.
3:
With the principle settled, David summons the nation, and the witnesses weigh the breadth of the gathering. The reformed John Gill takes "all Israel" to mean the chief and heads of the people, not every individual; and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fill in who these were — the elders, the rulers over thousands, and the heads of families, the same body that the older narrative at 2 Samuel 6:15 calls "all the house of Israel" taking part in the solemn march. The Chronicler, they note, first assembles the whole representative nation and then, in the verses following, gathers out from among them the heads of the priests and Levites to determine the details of the procession.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the verse beside its parallel and marks a small difference. Samuel says only that "David and all the house of Israel brought up the ark," without naming Jerusalem as the meeting-place; the Chronicler alone specifies the city, though of course only a full representation of the people can be meant, as at the earlier muster of 1 Chronicles 13:2 and 1 Chronicles 13:5. He adds a fine grammatical touch: the ark is to be brought "unto his place," for the neutral "its" was unknown to the older English of the Authorized Version, so that the sacred chest is spoken of almost as a person coming home.

Keil and Delitzsch look ahead to explain the order of the coming roster. "The sons of Aaron" will be the two high priests Zadok and Abiathar, and "the Levites" the six princes soon to be named, each with his brethren, answering to the three houses of Gershon, Kohath, and Merari into which the tribe was divided. Kohath, though not the eldest, is enumerated first, they explain, because Aaron himself sprang from Kohath, and because to the Kohathites fell the nearer and holier duty of bearing the most sacred vessels. The witnesses thus read the verse as the widening of the circle before it narrows: the whole nation is called to Jerusalem, that the ark may come to its prepared place borne on consecrated shoulders, and every rank of Israel may have its part in a joy the first attempt had turned to grief.
4:
The verse names the two orders David convened, and the witnesses treat it as the muster-roll's proper beginning. The reformed John Gill observes simply that David assembled the children of Aaron and the Levites, whose business it was to bear the ark whenever there was occasion, and that from this point the record gives an account of the Levites and their number employed in the work. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the picture: David confers separately with the priestly order about their part in the procession. The "children of Aaron" are the high priests Zadok and Abiathar, whom the king will address by name at v.11; and "the Levites" are the six chieftains about to be listed — Uriel, Asaiah, and Joel, heads of the clans of Kohath, Merari, and Gershom, together with Shemaiah, Eliel, and Amminadab, three further Kohathite chiefs, each standing at the head of his clansmen, the "brethren" of the verses that follow.

Ellicott draws out the arithmetic of the houses, and finds in it a memorial of ancient precedence. There are four Kohathite houses set against one apiece of Merari and Gershom, and the reason is that the sub-tribe of Kohath was the elder house in dignity, charged with the special care of the ark and the other most holy vessels of the sanctuary, according to the appointment of Numbers 4:4. So even the shape of the list preaches: the clan that bore the holiest burden is given the largest representation at the ark's homecoming.

The witnesses, few as they are on so plain a verse, agree on its function. It is the header to the census that follows, the moment David gathers the very men whose neglect at the first attempt had cost Israel dear, and now enlists them, house by house and chief by chief, for the duty that was theirs by divine right. The genealogical dryness of the roll, the expositors imply, is not idle antiquarianism; it is the Chronicler's way of showing that the second bringing-up of the ark was ordered down to the last family, everything done by the book, so that what had gone so fatally wrong might now go rightly.
5:
"Of the sons of Kohath; Uriel the chief, and his brethren an hundred and twenty." The census of the bearers begins, and the witnesses agree on why it begins here. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott states it in a phrase: Kohath comes first as the senior clan in dignity, the house to which the priestly line of Aaron itself belonged. Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, lay the whole roster out together, and set Kohath at its head for that very reason — Uriel its chief, with a hundred and twenty of his brethren mustered for the work.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley attends to the family tree behind the name. The "sons of Kohath" here are reckoned through Amram and Izhar, Kohath's own sons named in Numbers 3:27; and he notes that Elizaphan at v.8, Hebron at v.9, and Uzziel at v.10 were likewise of Kohath's line, so that four of the six houses in this list spring from the one senior clan. The reason the Kohathites should so dominate the roll is the reason Ellicott has already given: theirs was the charge of the ark itself, and it is fitting that the family entrusted of old with the holiest burden should furnish the greatest number to carry it home.

The witnesses find in the bare tally more than bookkeeping. That the record should begin with Kohath, and reckon its brethren by the hundred, is the Chronicler's quiet way of honouring the divine order of Numbers 4:4, where the sons of Kohath were set over "the most holy things." In the wilderness the Kohathites had marched in the heart of the camp, bearing the covered ark and its vessels upon the shoulder; and now, generations later, their sons resume that ancient office as the ark comes up to Zion. The precedence is not of birth — Gershon was Levi's firstborn — but of appointed service; and so the muster opens where the sanctity of the ark had always centred, among the Kohathites, whose one hundred and twenty men step first into the light of the procession that the rest of the chapter will describe.
7:
"Of the sons of Gershom; Joel the chief, and his brethren an hundred and thirty." The roll passes to the eldest branch of Levi, and the witnesses of the grammatical-historical school place the name in its family. Keil and Delitzsch identify Joel as the head of the Gershonites, the house descended from Gershon, Levi's firstborn son — one of the three great divisions of the tribe, answering to Gershon, Kohath, and Merari, the sons of Levi named in Exodus 6:16. Charles Ellicott, listing the six chieftains together, likewise sets Joel at the head of the clan of Gershom, mustering with him a hundred and thirty of his brethren for the bearing of the ark.

What the witnesses leave the reader to feel is the quiet reversal buried in the order of the list. Gershon was the firstborn of Levi, yet his house is not set first; the Kohathites, sprung from the second son, take precedence, because to them belonged the charge of the ark and the most holy things. So Joel and his Gershomites, senior by birth, are numbered here after the junior clan that carried the holier burden — a small witness to the truth that runs through the whole book, that place in the service of God is measured by appointment and not by natural priority.

Still, the Gershomites are not passed over. Their chief is named, their number counted, their brethren enrolled among those who would set their shoulders under the ark of the covenant. And it is these six princes, Joel among them, with all their reckoned brethren, whom David will presently charge to sanctify themselves for the sacred task, so that the eldest house of Levi takes its place among the consecrated bearers. The Chronicler, careful of every house, will not let the firstborn's branch go unrecorded in the day of the ark's homecoming; and the witnesses, grounding themselves only on what the roster plainly says, mark Joel the son of the eldest line taking his appointed station, his hundred and thirty men joined to the Kohathites and Merarites in the one hallowed labour that the earlier attempt had so disastrously left to unhallowed hands.
8:
"Of the sons of Elizaphan; Shemaiah the chief, and his brethren two hundred." The list returns to the house of Kohath by one of its younger branches, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott traces the descent. Elizaphan was a son of Uzziel, the fourth son of Kohath, as the family register of Exodus 6:18 and Exodus 6:22 records; and of this Kohathite house Shemaiah was the chief in David's own day, a man named again among the Levitical officers at 1 Chronicles 24:6. Keil and Delitzsch, gathering the whole roster into one view, count his brethren at two hundred, the largest company yet named.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley had already fixed Elizaphan's place in the tribe, reckoning him, with Hebron and Uzziel, among the children of Kohath — so that this house too, for all that it is listed apart under its own head, belongs to the senior clan whose charge was the ark. The effect, the witnesses imply, is that the Kohathites appear in the muster not as a single body but as four distinct houses, each with its chief and its counted brethren, as though the Chronicler would honour every sub-family by name. The same Shemaiah, Ellicott observes, reappears among the ordered Levites of a later day, so that a chief named here for a day's carriage of the ark is met again in the settled service of the sanctuary, his house no passing name but a fixture of Israel's worship.

The verse yields little for the expositor to dispute, and the witnesses do not strain it. What they do mark is the sheer weight of numbers the house of Elizaphan brought — two hundred men set apart for the one labour of carrying the ark and attending its progress. The genealogist's patience here is of a piece with his patience through the whole chapter: he means the reader to see that the second bringing-up of the ark was no hasty improvisation but a great ordered gathering of consecrated men, house by house, each answering to the roll, so that the sacred burden should be borne by many hands and every hand a Levite's, exactly as the law had required and the first attempt had so fatally ignored.
9:
"Of the sons of Hebron; Eliel the chief, and his brethren fourscore." Another Kohathite house steps forward, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott names its founder in the tribal line: Hebron was the third son of Kohath, as the genealogies of Exodus 6:18 and 1 Chronicles 6:2 set down. Keil and Delitzsch, reading the roster as a whole, give the house its chief and its number — Eliel at its head, with fourscore of his brethren, the smallest company among the six.

The witnesses spend few words on so slight a verse, but the little they imply is not without point. The house of Hebron musters only eighty men, where the house of Elizaphan brought two hundred and the Kohathites under Uriel a hundred and twenty; yet the Chronicler enrolls the lesser house as carefully as the greater, its chief named and its brethren counted. In the day of the ark's homecoming no branch of Levi is too small to be written down; the genealogist's exactness reaches to the fourscore of Hebron as surely as to the hundreds of the rest. Eighty men are a small company beside the greater houses, yet the record grants them the same dignity of naming, as if to teach that in the service of God the number of hands matters less than that every hand should be a sanctified one.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley, in reckoning the sons of Kohath, had already numbered Hebron among them, so that this house too is of the senior clan whose charge was the ark. The witnesses leave the verse as they found it — a single link in the long muster-roll — but they let it testify to the same care that governs the whole account. Eliel and his eighty stand among the consecrated bearers, their names preserved not for any deed recorded of them but simply because they answered the summons and took their appointed place under the sacred burden. The Chronicler, writing long after for a restored community anxious to know itself the true heir of the ancient tribe, counts even these, that the house of Hebron might not be forgotten in the register of those who brought the ark of God up to Zion.
10:
"Of the sons of Uzziel; Amminadab the chief, and his brethren an hundred and twelve." The last of the four Kohathite houses is named, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott untangles a knot in its descent. Uzziel was the fourth son of Kohath, as 1 Chronicles 6:2 records, and Exodus 6:22 names three sons of his — Mishael, Elzaphan, and Zithri. But the house of Elzaphan has already appeared in its own right at v.8; so the "sons of Uzziel" in this verse must represent the two remaining Uzzielite houses, which may by now have amalgamated into one. And since Elzaphan was reckoned first, Ellicott suggests, the elder line of Mishael may have died out, for the later lists at 1 Chronicles 23:20 and 1 Chronicles 24:24 imply the existence of only two Uzzielite stocks.

Keil and Delitzsch, taking the roster in one sweep, give the house its chief and tally — Amminadab, with a hundred and twelve of his brethren. With this the four Kohathite houses are complete: Uriel's hundred and twenty, Elizaphan's two hundred, Hebron's fourscore, and now Uzziel's hundred and twelve, all sprung from the one clan that bore the ark. Four houses, and well over five hundred men in all, are drawn from Kohath alone — a weight of numbers that answers to the weight of the charge, for it was the sons of Kohath who must lift the ark itself and not merely attend it.

The witnesses, sparing on the surface detail, let Ellicott's careful sorting stand as the verse's chief interest. The very obscurities of the genealogy — a house that seems to have merged with another, an elder line that appears to have failed — are for him evidence of the register's fidelity, the marks of a real family history and not a tidy invention. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley had likewise counted Uzziel among the children of Kohath, closing the circle of the senior clan. So the muster of the Kohathites ends where it must, with the ark's own tribe fully enrolled, every house named and numbered, that the holiest burden in Israel might be lifted by hands the law had sanctified and the Chronicler has taken pains to record.
11:
"And David called for Zadok and Abiathar the priests, and for the Levites." The king now addresses the leaders by name, and the witnesses gather around the standing of the two priests. The reformed John Gill notes only that David called for Zadok and Abiathar; but the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott makes them the eight spiritual chiefs of the enterprise and dwells on the pair at their head. Zadok and Abiathar were of coordinate rank, he holds, as representing the two lines of Eleazar and Ithamar, a parity he grounds in 1 Chronicles 6:4 and 1 Chronicles 24:3. Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, agree exactly: Zadok of the line of Eleazar and Abiathar of the line of Ithamar were the heads of the two priestly houses, and at that time both together held the office of high priest, as the notices of 2 Samuel 15:24 and 2 Samuel 20:25 confirm.

Against this the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley sets a quieter difference of rank: for him the priests are "Abiathar the high priest, and Zadok the second priest," not two of equal dignity but a chief and his deputy. So a small disagreement stands over the verse — whether Zadok and Abiathar shared the high priesthood as coordinate heads of the two lines, or whether Abiathar was the high priest and Zadok his second — the grammatical-historical witnesses holding the first, Wesley the second.

Ellicott finds a sermon hidden in the names of the men entrusted with so sacred a charge. Zadok means "just," akin to Zedekiah, "Jah is just"; Abiathar, "the Father excels"; Shemaiah, "Jah heareth"; Eliel, "God, and none else, is God"; Amminadab, "the Clansman is bounteous." Thus the very names of those who conducted this great religious event proclaimed, to themselves and to Israel, that Jehovah the Lord is righteous, that he alone is God, the author of all excellence and power, and that he hears the prayers of his people as a gracious Father. The witnesses leave the verse as a roll-call charged with meaning — a company of named men, priest and Levite, summoned to sanctify themselves and set their hands to the holy work.
12:
David's charge to the assembled chiefs is a call to holiness, and the witnesses read it as the heart of the reform. The reformed John Gill glosses the address plainly: "ye are the chief of the fathers of the Levites" means the heads of their fathers' houses, the men most responsible before God for the ark's right handling. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott expands the demand that follows. "Sanctify yourselves" points to the special purifications prescribed for all sacrifice and worship — bathing the person, washing or changing the garments, keeping aloof from every defilement — as at Genesis 35:2, Exodus 19:10, and Exodus 30:17; and all of it, he says, was needful to teach Israel that the All-pure requires purity in his worshippers, a lesson repeated at 2 Chronicles 30:3.

Ellicott hears a deliberate solemnity in the title David gives the ark. Where the earlier chapters spoke simply of "the ark of God," the king now says "the ark of the LORD God of Israel," a fuller and more warning form; and the phrase "God of Israel" marks the undertaking as national, so that the whole people's welfare hangs upon its due performance, as the principle of 1 Samuel 2:30 would suggest. Israel's very vocation, he adds, was to be "a kingdom of priests, and an holy nation," the calling of Exodus 19:6 which the Chronicler has well understood.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch join the charge to its ground in the verse that follows: the leaders must sanctify themselves "because in the beginning it was not you" who brought up the ark, and therefore the LORD made a breach upon Israel by the slaying of Uzza. The command to be holy, then, is no mere ceremony but the correction of a fatal negligence. The witnesses converge on the seriousness of it: those advanced above others in dignity must go before them in duty; the chief of the fathers are told to cleanse themselves first, that the ark long dishonoured by a careless hand might now be carried by men set apart and made clean for the God whose holiness had already once broken forth against them.
13:
"For because ye did it not at the first, the LORD our God made a breach upon us." The verse looks back to Uzza, and the witnesses probe both its cramped Hebrew and its hard theology. The reformed John Gill corrects the drift of the Authorized supplement: the sense is not that the Levites failed to sanctify themselves, but that "ye were not there at first" — when the ark was brought from Kirjathjearim as far as the house of Obededom, the Levites were not employed in the work that was properly theirs. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the clause the same way: "because on the first occasion it was not you" — the heads of the Levitical houses — who carried up the ark, but Uzza and Ahio, the sons of Abinadab of 2 Samuel 6:3; and he notes that the odd phrase so rendered occurs nowhere else in Scripture.

On "made a breach" the witnesses agree. Ellicott glosses it "broke out upon us," the same expression used at Exodus 19:22, referring to the sudden death of Uzza recorded at 1 Chronicles 13:10. And the fault confessed in "we sought him not after the due order" was precisely this: the ark had been set on a cart instead of being borne by the sons of Kohath "upon their shoulders, with the staves thereon," as v.15 and Numbers 4:15 required — for even the Kohathites themselves were forbidden to touch the holy thing, as Uzza had rashly done.

Out of this Ellicott draws a larger reflection, and the witnesses let it stand as the verse's abiding lesson. There is a "due order" by which God rules the physical world, and a corresponding order by which his will is fulfilled in the spiritual sphere; the sanctity of divine institutions is no arbitrary thing. If we abolish the divine authority of positive institutions under the gospel, he asks, on what ground do we keep them under Moses? So the death of Uzza, confessed here by a humbled king, becomes a standing warning that God will be approached in his own appointed way, and that a reverence which neglects his order is not reverence but presumption.
14:
"So the priests and the Levites sanctified themselves to bring up the ark of the LORD God of Israel." The command of the previous verses is obeyed, and the witnesses mark the obedience as the turning of the whole account. The reformed John Gill notes only that they sanctified themselves as they were ordered, and in the manner they should — the bare compliance carrying, for him, the weight of a lesson learned. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch join the verse to the act it prepares: the Levites consecrated themselves and then bore, as the next verse anticipates, the ark of God upon their shoulders, according to the prescription of Numbers 7:9, by means of the poles laid upon their shoulders — the flexible carrying-pole named in Numbers 13:23, the very staves called "bars" in Exodus 25:13.

The witnesses find the significance of the verse less in its detail than in its placement. It stands as the answer to the breach upon Uzza: where careless hands and an unhallowed cart had once provoked the wrath of God, now sanctified men prepare themselves in the appointed way. Gill's terse remark that they sanctified themselves "in the manner they should" is itself the point — the manner had been wanting the first time, and everything had gone wrong; now the manner is right, and the ark will come up in peace.

Keil and Delitzsch pass at once from the consecration to the music, for the same verse leads into David's charge that the chief of the Levites appoint singers with instruments — psalteries, harps, and cymbals — to lift up the voice with joy. So the witnesses see the two halves of the ark's right service joined in a single breath: first the men are made clean, and then they are made glad; the purity that the law required and the praise that the occasion called for are set side by side, the one the ground of the other. The bearers are sanctified before they are festal, that the joy of Israel might rise from hands and hearts God had first made holy.
15:
"And the children of the Levites bare the ark of God upon their shoulders, with the staves thereon, as Moses commanded." The verse records the law fulfilled at last, and the witnesses dwell on the manner of the carrying. The reformed John Gill stresses that they bore it not only on their shoulders but with the staves in it, resting on their shoulders; and he passes on the reason Maimonides gave for the command that the ark be carried by men and never in any wagon — both for the honour of it, and lest its form and structure should in any way be damaged. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke pictures the mechanism exactly: the staves which went through the rings rested on the bearers' shoulders, while the ark itself rested upon the staves, like a sedan borne upon its poles, all according to Numbers 4:15.

The grammatical-historical witnesses ground the act in the Mosaic word. Keil and Delitzsch cite the prescription of Numbers 7:9 and Exodus 25:13, that the sons of Kohath should bear the holy things upon the shoulder with the poles thereon. Charles Ellicott renders the Hebrew closely — "with their shoulder, with the poles upon themselves" — and observes that this statement in fact anticipates the procession described in full only from v.25; such brief anticipative summaries, he notes, set down before the detailed narrative, are a common feature of Hebrew storytelling.

What the witnesses will not let pass is the phrase "as Moses commanded according to the word of the LORD." The whole reform of the chapter is gathered into it. The disaster of the first attempt had lain in doing a good thing in a wrong way; now the same ark is moved, but every particular answers to the ancient command — borne, not carted; on the shoulder, not on the ox-cart; by Levites, not by whoever came to hand. The expositors read the verse as the visible vindication of God's order: obedience in the small matter of the staves is the sign of a people who have learned that the God who dwells between the cherubim must be served as he has himself directed, and in no other way.
16:
David turns from the bearers to the singers, and the witnesses follow him into the ordering of the temple music. The reformed John Gill notes simply that the king spoke to the chief of the Levites — the six princes already named — to appoint their brethren as singers. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the charge more exactly: David ordered the chiefs to station their clansmen, the minstrels, with harps and lutes — the nebalim and kinnoroth of the Hebrew — and with cymbals. The participle "sounding," he argues, belongs best to the cymbals alone, given the special force of the verb, which recurs of the "loud cymbals" of Psalms 150:5; so he would translate, "harps and lutes and clashing cymbals, in order to swell the sound for gladness," comparing the great chorus of 2 Chronicles 5:13.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, describe the three kinds of instrument named — the nablia or psaltery, an instrument of stretched wires something like the cithara; the kinnor, more lute than harp; and the cymbals — all appointed to accompany the song. The witnesses agree that David is here founding an institution, not merely arranging a day's ceremony.

That larger point the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses home. This way of praising God by musical instruments, he observes, had not hitherto been in use; but David, being a prophet, instituted it by divine direction and added it to the other ordinances of that dispensation. Every man was to minister according to the gift he had received, the rule of 1 Peter 4:10; those who excelled in any endowment were not to keep it to themselves but to use it for the common good and teach others too. Henry is careful, however, of the bounds of the precedent: the New Testament keeps up the singing of psalms but has not appointed church-music, ranking the instruments among those carnal ordinances of the law that Hebrews 9:10 names. So the witnesses leave the verse as the charter of sacred song — joy commanded, gifts enlisted, and the whole service of praise ordered by the prophet-king for the coming of the ark.
17:
"So the Levites appointed Heman the son of Joel; and of his brethren, Asaph the son of Berechiah; and of the sons of Merari their brethren, Ethan the son of Kushaiah." The three great precentors are named, and the witnesses set them in their lineage. The reformed John Gill marks Heman as the son of Joel and grandson of Samuel the prophet, a descent that gave the chief of the singers a prophetic pedigree. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke identifies the trio as the three chief musicians of David's day, pointing to their fuller genealogies at 1 Chronicles 6:31.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws out what is most telling in the appointment: Heman, Asaph, and Ethan — the last also called Jeduthun — were the precentors of David's three choirs of Levitical minstrels, and each sprang from a different house of the tribe. Heman was of Kohath, Asaph of Gershom, and Ethan of Merari, so that the three branches of Levi were represented, one leader from each, as the register of 1 Chronicles 6:33 and the verses following it show. Keil and Delitzsch, reading the roster as a whole, describe how these three, with their second-rank brethren, formed the choirs that would sound the cymbals, the psalteries, and the harps in the procession.

The witnesses find in that threefold division a fitting harmony. The one tribe of Levi, split of old into the houses of Gershom, Kohath, and Merari, is here reunited in a single act of praise, each house giving its master-singer to lead the music before the ark. What birth and genealogy had separated, worship gathers again; the sons of the three brothers stand together at the head of the choirs, that the whole tribe might have its voice in the joy of the ark's return. The Chronicler, ever attentive to the orders of the Levites, records the appointment not as a bare fact of administration but as the founding of that sacred song which would sound in Israel long after, when the tent David pitched had given way to the temple, and Heman, Asaph, and Ethan had become the fathers of the singing guilds of the house of God.
18:
"And with them their brethren of the second degree." The record passes to the lesser singers and the doorkeepers, and the witnesses attend to the ranks and the tangled names. The reformed John Gill explains "the second degree" as those next to the three chief musicians, both in office and in skill of singing. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees, taking the first rank to be the three famous men of v.17 and these the next below them; and he notes that the porters here named, though set to keep the doors of the tabernacle and its courts, were withal instructed in music, that when free from their own office they might not be idle in the house of God.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works through the list with a text-critic's care. These fourteen are the minstrels of the second rank, subordinate to the first three, as the phrase "his second Zechariah" at 1 Chronicles 16:5 confirms. But the word "Ben," he insists, is not a proper name at all: it is simply the Hebrew for "son," and the name of Zechariah's father has probably fallen out after it, or a scribe has set it down by inadvertence; the Septuagint omits it altogether. So a small dispute stands over the reading — whether "Ben" is a singer's name or a fragment left by a slip of the pen — with Ellicott and the versions against treating it as a person.

Ellicott lingers, too, over the strange name Shemiramoth, which resembles the Assyrian Sammurramat, the classical Semiramis; Delitzsch, he reports, would explain it as a compound meaning "loving spices," a name actually borne by a lady of the Assyrian court about eight centuries before Christ. And he corrects "Jaaziel" here against the "Jeiel" of 1 Chronicles 16:5, taking the one as a slip for the other. The witnesses thus leave the verse as a bruised but recoverable register: a double band of second-rank singers and warders of the ark, their names worn by copying, yet each meant to be preserved, down to the humblest doorkeeper, in the roll of those who served at the ark's homecoming.
19:
"So the singers, Heman, Asaph, and Ethan, were appointed to sound with cymbals of brass." The three precentors take up their instruments, and the witnesses read the verse as the head of the procession's order. The reformed John Gill notes only that these were the three principal singers, set apart for the cymbals. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the phrase more sharply — "with cymbals of bronze for clashing" — and explains their office: furnished with these loud instruments, the three chiefs were to lead and accentuate the music, marking its time for the rest.

Ellicott finds in this verse and the ones around it the very marshalling of the great march, and lays it out in order. First came the three master-singers with their brazen cymbals, and behind them two bands of seven each, with psalteries and harps; then Chenaniah, the marshal of the bearers; then two warders of the ark; and last seven priests with their trumpets. Keil and Delitzsch, reading the same roster, describe how the singers formed three choirs according to their instruments, the cymbals of Heman, Asaph, and Ethan sounding above the psalteries and lutes of the rest. The bronze of the cymbals, harder and more ringing than the softer strings, gave the leaders a sound that would not be lost in the tumult of a rejoicing nation.

The witnesses spend little dispute on so plain a notice, but they let its arrangement speak. The cymbals of brass, given to the chief singers, were the instruments that carried furthest and cut through the noise of a great multitude; it is fitting that the leaders of the music should hold them, setting the beat by which the whole company moved and sang. The Chronicler, careful to name not only the bearers of the ark but the makers of its music, shows the second bringing-up of the ark as an ordered and resounding thing — every rank in its place, the loudest instruments in the surest hands — so that the joy of Israel should rise not in confusion but in measured and mighty praise, led by the three whose names would stand at the head of the temple's song for generations to come.
20:
"And Zechariah, and Aziel, and Shemiramoth... with psalteries on Alamoth." The first band of second-rank musicians is named, and the witnesses divide over both a missing man and a musical term. The reformed John Gill counts eight of the second degree here, notes that Aziel is the same as the Jaaziel of v.18, and observes that "Ben" has dropped out, with Azaziah seemingly put in his room at v.21; he passes on the fancy of Kimchi's father that this Ben, thought to have sickened or died before he could serve, is the one named in the psalm-title "upon Muthlabben," the death of Ben.

The chief interest, though, is the phrase "on Alamoth," and here the witnesses genuinely part. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes it to mean "after the mode of maidens," that is, of soprano compass or pitch, the same expression that stands in the heading of Psalms 46. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports two other guesses: that "Alamoth" denotes virgins or women singers, whom these players were to accompany and lead, or, following the Vulgate's "arcana cantabant," that they "sang secret things," mysteries, probably prophetic hymns. John Wesley, of the same school, confesses the honest truth that undercuts them all: "Alamoth" is thought to be the name of an instrument, or of a certain tune or note or part in music, but its certain signification is not now known — and the like, he adds, must be said of "Sheminith" in the next verse.

So the debate stands unresolved among the witnesses: soprano voices, a company of maidens, a mode of secret song, or a lost technical term whose meaning has perished. Keil and Delitzsch, gathering the roster, are content to note that this band played the psalteries "on Alamoth" while the next played harps "on the Sheminith," and that these Hebrew words plainly mark different keys or manners of singing, obscure to us only because so little of the music of the Hebrews survives. The witnesses leave the verse as a window into a vanished art — a second choir of named men, their instruments and their mode set down with a precision the ages have since half-erased.
21:
"And Mattithiah, and Elipheleh... with harps on the Sheminith to excel." The second band of players is enrolled, and the witnesses take up the two puzzling terms that describe their music. The reformed John Gill notes simply that these six were appointed to sound. On "the Sheminith" the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gathers the old conjectures: the Targum took it for an instrument sounding an octave, or one of eight strings; the Syriac and Arabic made it instruments to sing with daily, at the third, sixth, and ninth hour; the Vulgate rendered it "an octave, for a song of victory"; while some supposed it named the eighth band of the musicians, those with the strongest and most sonorous voices.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott cuts through to a musical sense: "with lutes in the bass," literally "after the mode of the eighth," an octave below the tenor — al ottava bassa. And "to excel," he explains, means to lead the orchestra, to precent, the very word that stands in the heading of Psalms 6. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads "to excel" a little differently, taking it to note either the excellency of that instrument or part, or a greater extension and elevation of the voice than in the band before; and he repeats, of "Sheminith" as of "Alamoth," that its certain meaning is now lost.

So the witnesses leave "the Sheminith" much as they left "Alamoth" — an octave, a bass register, an eight-stringed instrument, a band of deep voices, or a term simply beyond recovery. Yet Wesley draws from the whole arrangement a point on which the schools agree: this way of praising God by musical instruments had not been in use before, but David instituted it by divine direction and added it to the ordinances of that dispensation. Whatever the exact pitch of the Sheminith, the witnesses see behind the technical words a real and ordered music — bands assigned their instruments, leaders set to precent, high voices and low ranged in their parts — all newly framed by the prophet-king to carry the joy of Israel before the ark of God.
22:
"And Chenaniah, chief of the Levites, was for song: he instructed about the song, because he was skilful." The verse names an officer of the procession, and over his office the witnesses fall into their sharpest disagreement of the chapter. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke makes him the master singer, who "presided in the elevation" — that is, gave the key and the time, pitching the tune for the choirs; skilful in music and powerful of voice, he was well qualified to lead the band, and might well have been the precentor. John Wesley, of the same school, agrees: Chenaniah was the moderator of the music, instructing the singers when and how to lift up their voices, change their notes, or make their stops.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dissents flatly, and would render the verse, "Chenaniah, captain of the Levites in the bearing, was conducting the bearing, because he was skilled" — skilled, that is, in the traditional regulations for carrying the ark duly and rightly. He grounds this on three considerations: the context, which distinguishes him from the singers Heman, Asaph, and Ethan; the fact that Chenaniah is nowhere else associated with music, but is named again only among the officers over the outward business at 1 Chronicles 26:29; and the sense of the Hebrew word massa, "bearing," which carries just this meaning of Levitical carriage at Numbers 4:19 and 2 Chronicles 35:3. He notes too that Chenaniah is called "the master of the carriage" again at v.27.

So a real debate stands over Chenaniah: was he the conductor of the song or the marshal of the ark's carriage? The wesleyan-arminian witnesses, with the Septuagint's "leader of the songs," make him precentor; the grammatical-historical Ellicott, with the Syriac's "bore the burden," makes him master of the bearing. The reformed John Gill stands between, allowing that "the chief for the song" were rather Heman, Asaph, and Ethan, and so leaning away from Clarke's reading. Even the clause "he instructed" is ambiguous, Ellicott adds, its Hebrew form open to "instructing" or "being prince over." The witnesses cannot finally settle whether Chenaniah lifted the tune or ordered the march; what they grant alike is that he was a skilled man set over a sacred and exacting task, whichever it was.
23:
"And Berechiah, and Elkanah, were doorkeepers for the ark." The record turns to the wardens who guarded the sacred chest, and the witnesses weigh where and how they served. The reformed John Gill offers three possibilities — that they kept the door while the ark stood at Kirjathjearim and at Obededom's house, or after it was set in the tent David had prepared, or, best of all, that as it was being carried they went before it to keep the people from pressing upon it or gazing at it. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley settles firmly on the last: they were appointed to keep the door of the tent in which the ark was to rest, that no unallowed person might press in and touch it, and likewise to attend upon the ark along the way and guard it from the throng and the touch of profane hands.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the office the same way, glossing "doorkeepers" as warders, the same men called "porters" in the earlier list; Obededom and Jehiah were warders of the ark as well, named at v.24, so that in the procession two may have walked before the ark and two behind, all responsible to prevent any unauthorized approach to the ark of God. Keil and Delitzsch, taking the closing verses together, simply set these doorkeepers in their place within the marching order that leads on to the trumpeters and the great company of v.25.

The witnesses find in this small office a large caution. The breach upon Uzza had come of an unguarded touch; now men are set on purpose to see that no such thing occur again, walking sentinel before and behind the ark that had already once broken forth in wrath. Wesley's phrase "the touch of profane hands" catches the whole burden of the chapter: the ark is holy, and its holiness must be fenced, so that the joy of its coming might not turn a second time to sorrow. Even the doorkeepers, the witnesses imply, are ministers of that reverence which the first attempt had lacked and the second was resolved to keep.
24:
"And Shebaniah, and Jehoshaphat... the priests, did blow with the trumpets before the ark of God: and Obededom and Jehiah were doorkeepers for the ark." The verse ranks the trumpeting priests and the rear wardens, and the witnesses attend to both. The reformed John Gill identifies the instruments as the silver trumpets, one use of which was to be blown when the camps and the ark of the Lord set forward. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott counts seven priestly trumpeters and renders "did blow" as "were blowing"; the pair of silver clarions, he notes from Numbers 10:2, were blown by the priests for the calling of the assembly and the journeying of the camps, and are named again at 1 Chronicles 16:6; the seven, he suggests, may have walked immediately before the ark, as the seven priests did at Jericho in Joshua 6:4.

Over the closing names a small question arises. Obededom and Jehiah are here made doorkeepers of the ark, and Ellicott doubts they are the same as the minstrels Obededom and Jeiel of the earlier verses. He gives three reasons: the order of the procession, in which two doorkeepers walked before the ark and two behind, while the minstrel Obededom played his lute several places ahead; the difference between the names "Jeiel" and "Jehiah," which means "Jah liveth"; and the sheer frequency with which names recur in these lists, so that a second Obededom need not surprise us, as the notice of 1 Chronicles 16:38 may show.

So the witnesses leave a mild disagreement over whether one Obededom served in two capacities or two men bore the name; but they converge on the shape of the whole. The ark advances hedged about with reverence and sound — priests before it with the God-appointed trumpets that had once summoned Israel to march, warders behind it to keep off every unhallowed hand. Gill's plain note that the silver trumpets sounded "when the ark moved" ties the scene to the old wilderness order: what Moses had ordained for the camps in the desert is now fulfilled as the ark comes home to Zion, every office filled and every instrument in its appointed place.
25:
The preparations are complete, and with this verse the great procession at last sets forth. The reformed John Gill takes "David and the elders of Israel and the captains over thousands" for the king and the principal men of the nation and the officers of its militia — the whole leadership of Israel gathered to fetch the ark from the house of Obededom. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the Hebrew as a set scene: "it was David and the elders of Israel and the princes of the thousands who were walking to bring up the ark," and notes that the parallel at 2 Samuel 6:12 makes no mention of the elders and captains, but says briefly only that David went and brought up the ark because of the blessing on Obededom's house.

Ellicott marks a title that may betray the Chronicler's sources. "The ark of the covenant of the LORD" is a special name for the ark not found in the parallel passage of Samuel, which has only "the ark of God" and "the ark of Jehovah"; the fuller phrase, met also at Joshua 3:3, may indicate that the writer drew on another record besides the book of Samuel. And "with joy," he adds, means with set rejoicings and festal mirth — the very note the first attempt had ended without.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the verse into worship. All being ready, the ark is brought up from Obededom's house with every expression of holy gladness; and Henry bids us take notice of the assistance of divine providence even in things that fall within the compass of our natural powers — for the Levites, remembering the breach upon Uzza, were ready to tremble as they took up the ark, until God helped them, encouraging them, silencing their fears, and strengthening their faith, as the confession of Acts 26:22 owns all our sufficiency to be from God. Keil and Delitzsch simply join the verse to what follows, where the helped Levites offer their sacrifice. The witnesses leave David and all the great ones of Israel on the road at last, walking the ark home in a joy grounded not in their own strength but in the God who steadied the trembling hands beneath the sacred burden.
26:
"And it came to pass, when God helped the Levites that bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, that they offered seven bullocks and seven rams." The witnesses read the verse as gratitude answering grace. The reformed John Gill will not have "helped" mean mere bodily strength, which the light burden scarcely needed; God helped the Levites to carry the ark with cheerfulness and without fear, so as to commit no error nor in any way provoke his displeasure, as they had done when it was first brought from Kirjathjearim. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the sacrifice as thanksgiving: when they saw that God had made no breach among them, as he had upon Uzza, in gratitude for their preservation and his acceptance of their labour they offered seven bullocks and seven rams. John Wesley adds that God "helped" them by encouraging them with some comfortable sign of his presence, and draws the lesson that in all our religious exercises we must derive help from heaven, and that God's ministers who bear the vessels of the Lord have special need of divine help in their ministrations.

The grammatical-historical witnesses set the offering beside its parallel and find a difficulty. Keil and Delitzsch note that the seven bullocks and seven rams were offered after the journey was happily accomplished, whereas 2 Samuel 6:13 mentions rather the offering made at the very start, when the bearers had gone but six paces. Charles Ellicott weighs whether the two accounts describe the same sacrifice or two different ones. Some, he reports, would have Samuel mean that a bullock and a fatling were slain at every six paces by priests posted along the route, and our text a single final offering at the ark's arrival — a solution he finds incredible, not least for its unmentioned priests. He judges it far likelier that the two notices are of one sacrifice, the Chronicler having paraphrased the older text for clearness, and giving, more exactly than Samuel, the number and kind of the victims, as at Numbers 23:1.

So the witnesses leave a quiet debate — one offering variously told, or two offerings at start and finish — but they agree on its meaning. God had been adverse at the first attempt, as the death of Uzza at 1 Chronicles 13:9 had shown; now, the ark borne in due order without a sign of wrath, the relieved hearts of Israel pour out their bullocks and rams in praise. The sacrifice is the confession of a people who know their safety to be sheer mercy, and their success in the holy work to be the help of God and not the strength of their own shoulders.
27:
"And David was clothed with a robe of fine linen... David also had upon him an ephod of linen." The verse dresses the king for worship, and the witnesses find in it both a textual riddle and a lesson in humility. The reformed John Gill notes that David, though a king, was clothed as the singers were, being among them — the royal robes laid aside for the linen of a minister. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke describes the stuff: the robe was of buts, or byssus, a rich fabric he takes to have been spun from the beard of the Pinna Magna, a great Mediterranean shellfish, of a deep dark yellow inclining to lilac; and he remarks that this byssus was not heard of in Israel before David's time, and is often mentioned after it.

The grammatical-historical witnesses fasten on the difference from the parallel. Where our verse says David was "clothed with a robe of byssus," 2 Samuel 6:14 reads that "David danced before Jehovah with all his might." Charles Ellicott judges the one a corruption or intentional alteration of the other: the word for "clothed" is the Aramaic mekurbal of Daniel 3:21, which might easily have been substituted, by accident or design, for the rare word mekarker, "dancing." Keil and Delitzsch reason the same way, noting that the two Hebrew words differ only in their last letters, so that the one may well have arisen out of the other. So a genuine textual debate stands: did the ancient text tell of David dancing or of David clothed in linen — and has one reading quietly displaced the other?

Ellicott adds two further touches. "The master of the song" he would render again "the chief of the bearing," as at v.22, keeping to his reading of Chenaniah's office; and he takes the me'il to be the upper garment of persons of rank, worn also by the Levites and singers, as at Job 29:14. Over it David wore the linen ephod, and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley marks why this is repeated: it was an unusual thing for one who was no Levite to wear a Levitical garment. The witnesses thus leave the reader with a king stripped of his majesty and girt like a priest — whether dancing or robed, the point is the same — a monarch content to be least before the ark, wearing the plain linen of God's servants in the sight of all his people.
28:
"Thus all Israel brought up the ark of the covenant of the LORD with shouting, and with sound of the cornet, and with trumpets, and with cymbals, making a noise with psalteries and harps." The verse gathers the whole procession into one great sound, and the witnesses mark chiefly how the Chronicler has enlarged the older account. The reformed John Gill passes over it briefly, referring the reader back to the parallel. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that this verse, set against 2 Samuel 6:5, is somewhat enlarged by the enumeration of the individual instruments — the writer naming one by one what the earlier record had left in a phrase.

Charles Ellicott renders the opening "and all Israel were bringing up the ark," and notes that Samuel has instead "David and all the house of Israel," and "the ark of the LORD" for the fuller "ark of the covenant." The "cornet," he corrects, is rather a trumpet; the rest of the verse is wanting in Samuel, though all the added instruments have already been named in the appointing of the singers; and the last clause he would render "with clanging cymbals, with harps and lutes," comparing the summons of Psalms 150:3 and the verse that follows it.

The witnesses do not dispute the verse; they let its music speak. Where the first attempt had ended in a stroke of death and a frightened silence, the second comes home in a storm of joy — the shout of the people, the blast of the trumpets, the clash of the cymbals, the strings of psaltery and harp all sounding together. The Chronicler's very fullness, cataloguing each instrument in turn, is itself a kind of praise: he will not compress into a word what deserves to be told at length. All Israel, and not David alone, brings up the ark; the whole nation that had been gathered to Jerusalem now lifts the sacred chest to its resting-place with every voice and every instrument it has, in the ordered and exultant clamour that the law's due order had at last made safe.
29:
"And it came to pass, as the ark of the covenant of the LORD came to the city of David, that Michal the daughter of Saul looking out at a window saw king David dancing and playing: and she despised him in her heart." The chapter closes on a note of discord, and the witnesses read the queen's contempt against the king's abandon. The reformed John Gill sets the scene at the palace window from which Michal watched the procession, and marks the inward scorn she conceived at David's behaviour, referring the reader to the fuller account of what she said and what David answered at 2 Samuel 6:20.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the verse as a modernised form of 2 Samuel 6:16, and renders it closely: the ark had come so far as to the city, and Michal had looked forth by the lattice, and she saw the king. For the two obsolete words that Samuel uses of David's motion, he notes, the Chronicler has put two common ones, "dancing and playing"; and "playing" here denotes dancing joined with singing and instruments, as in Jeremiah 30:19 and 1 Samuel 18:6. Why she despised him he states without flinching: because David seemed to her forgetful of his royal and manly honour, dancing like a woman before the people. Keil and Delitzsch likewise note that the Chronicler here explains a few of the older words, but for the rest refers his reader to the history in Samuel, where the whole exchange is told.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke does the same, pointing to 2 Samuel 6:20 where David's conduct is vindicated and the nature of Michal's disgrace and punishment is hinted at, though left to the reader's own weighing. So the witnesses gather around a single, poignant contrast, and do not really divide. On the one side is David, a king who has laid aside his majesty to leap and sing before the ark, counting no abasement too low in the joy of that day; on the other is Saul's daughter, watching from above, measuring the scene by the dignity of a throne and finding her husband contemptible. The ark comes home in triumph, and at the very threshold of its rest the old house of Saul and the new heart of David are set side by side — the one despising, the other dancing — so that the chapter of ordered joy ends, tellingly, on the one heart in Israel that could not rejoice.
